verses.text: Famine reaches Canaan. Jacob hears there is grain in Egypt and tells his sons: why are you looking at each other? Go, buy us food before we die.
Famine reaches Canaan. Jacob hears there is grain in Egypt and tells his sons: why are you looking at each other? Go, buy us food before we die.

The ten older sons go down. Jacob does not send Benjamin; he is afraid harm might come to him. The brothers travel with the rest of the world toward the storehouses, not knowing where they are walking.

The ten older sons go down. Jacob does not send Benjamin; he is afraid harm might come to him. The brothers travel with the rest of the world toward the storehouses, not knowing where they are walking.

The ten older sons go down. Jacob does not send Benjamin; he is afraid harm might come to him. The brothers travel with the rest of the world toward the storehouses, not knowing where they are walking.

Joseph is governor over the land — the one who sells grain to everyone. His brothers come and bow themselves before him, faces to the ground. The dreams of chapter 37 are fulfilled, but only one person in the room knows it. He speaks roughly: where do you come from? Canaan.

Joseph is governor over the land — the one who sells grain to everyone. His brothers come and bow themselves before him, faces to the ground. The dreams of chapter 37 are fulfilled, but only one person in the room knows it. He speaks roughly: where do you come from? Canaan.

Joseph recognized his brothers, but they did not recognize him. He remembered the dreams he had once dreamed. You are spies, he says — you have come to see the nakedness of the land. The accusation is harsh; it is meant to be.

Joseph recognized his brothers, but they did not recognize him. He remembered the dreams he had once dreamed. You are spies, he says — you have come to see the nakedness of the land. The accusation is harsh; it is meant to be.

No, my lord, they protest — we have come only to buy food. We are all sons of one man. Joseph repeats the accusation. Your servants are twelve brothers, they say, sons of one man in Canaan — the youngest is with our father, and one is no more. They mention Joseph without knowing him.

No, my lord, they protest — we have come only to buy food. We are all sons of one man. Joseph repeats the accusation. Your servants are twelve brothers, they say, sons of one man in Canaan — the youngest is with our father, and one is no more. They mention Joseph without knowing him.

No, my lord, they protest — we have come only to buy food. We are all sons of one man. Joseph repeats the accusation. Your servants are twelve brothers, they say, sons of one man in Canaan — the youngest is with our father, and one is no more. They mention Joseph without knowing him.

No, my lord, they protest — we have come only to buy food. We are all sons of one man. Joseph repeats the accusation. Your servants are twelve brothers, they say, sons of one man in Canaan — the youngest is with our father, and one is no more. They mention Joseph without knowing him.

That is what I said — you are spies. By Pharaoh's life, you will not leave here unless your youngest brother comes. Send one of you to fetch him; the rest will stay in prison until your words are tested. He puts them all in custody for three days.

That is what I said — you are spies. By Pharaoh's life, you will not leave here unless your youngest brother comes. Send one of you to fetch him; the rest will stay in prison until your words are tested. He puts them all in custody for three days.

That is what I said — you are spies. By Pharaoh's life, you will not leave here unless your youngest brother comes. Send one of you to fetch him; the rest will stay in prison until your words are tested. He puts them all in custody for three days.

That is what I said — you are spies. By Pharaoh's life, you will not leave here unless your youngest brother comes. Send one of you to fetch him; the rest will stay in prison until your words are tested. He puts them all in custody for three days.

On the third day Joseph speaks again: do this and live, for I fear God. If you are honest men, let one brother stay in prison; the rest take grain home and bring the youngest. So your words will be verified, and you will not die. They agreed.

On the third day Joseph speaks again: do this and live, for I fear God. If you are honest men, let one brother stay in prison; the rest take grain home and bring the youngest. So your words will be verified, and you will not die. They agreed.

On the third day Joseph speaks again: do this and live, for I fear God. If you are honest men, let one brother stay in prison; the rest take grain home and bring the youngest. So your words will be verified, and you will not die. They agreed.

And among themselves they say: we are guilty about our brother — we saw the distress of his soul when he begged us, and we would not listen; that is why this trouble has come on us. Reuben adds: I told you not to harm the child. Now his blood is required. Twenty years of guilt finally speaks out loud.

And among themselves they say: we are guilty about our brother — we saw the distress of his soul when he begged us, and we would not listen; that is why this trouble has come on us. Reuben adds: I told you not to harm the child. Now his blood is required. Twenty years of guilt finally speaks out loud.
They did not know Joseph understood them; he had been speaking through an interpreter all along. Every word of their confession was overheard.

Joseph turns away and weeps. Then he returns, takes Simeon — the second oldest — and binds him before their eyes. The brother who once threw him in a pit is now bound himself. The justice is slow but symmetrical.

Joseph orders their sacks filled with grain, each man's money restored to his own sack, and provisions given for the road. None of this is told to the brothers. They load up the donkeys and head home.

Joseph orders their sacks filled with grain, each man's money restored to his own sack, and provisions given for the road. None of this is told to the brothers. They load up the donkeys and head home.

At a lodging place, one brother opens his sack to feed his donkey and sees his money in the mouth of the sack. He shows the others. Their hearts sink. They tremble at one another: what is this that God has done to us? Strange mercy reads to a guilty heart like a snare.

At a lodging place, one brother opens his sack to feed his donkey and sees his money in the mouth of the sack. He shows the others. Their hearts sink. They tremble at one another: what is this that God has done to us? Strange mercy reads to a guilty heart like a snare.
They reach Jacob and tell him everything. The man who is lord of the land spoke roughly to us, took us for spies. We told him we were honest men, twelve brothers, sons of one father.
They reach Jacob and tell him everything. The man who is lord of the land spoke roughly to us, took us for spies. We told him we were honest men, twelve brothers, sons of one father.
They reach Jacob and tell him everything. The man who is lord of the land spoke roughly to us, took us for spies. We told him we were honest men, twelve brothers, sons of one father.

Twelve brothers, we said — one is no more, the youngest is with our father. The man told us he would test us: leave one with him, bring the youngest, and he would believe us and let us trade in the land. They retell the demand to their father, anxiously.

Twelve brothers, we said — one is no more, the youngest is with our father. The man told us he would test us: leave one with him, bring the youngest, and he would believe us and let us trade in the land. They retell the demand to their father, anxiously.

Twelve brothers, we said — one is no more, the youngest is with our father. The man told us he would test us: leave one with him, bring the youngest, and he would believe us and let us trade in the land. They retell the demand to their father, anxiously.
Then they empty their sacks — and there in every man's sack is his own bundle of money. They are afraid. Their father is afraid. The day's news keeps adding up wrong.

Jacob: you have bereaved me of my children. Joseph is not. Simeon is not. And now you would take Benjamin. All these things are against me. Reuben offers: kill my two sons if I do not bring him back. Put him in my hand. The promise lands without comforting.

Jacob: you have bereaved me of my children. Joseph is not. Simeon is not. And now you would take Benjamin. All these things are against me. Reuben offers: kill my two sons if I do not bring him back. Put him in my hand. The promise lands without comforting.

My son shall not go down with you, says Jacob. His brother is dead, and he alone is left. If harm came to him on the journey, you would bring my gray hairs down to the grave with sorrow. The favorite wife's only remaining son is not negotiable. Yet.
